title: Understanding rural–urban transitions in the Global South through peri-urban turbulence
data_sources: IEA/World Bank Tracking SDG 7 Energy Progress Report
Cattaneo et al. PNAS 2021 (global urban-rural catchment mapping)

data_scope.paper_type: Perspective / theoretical review (no new primary dataset)
data_scope.peri_urban_population_cited: approximately 1 billion people globally in 2015 (from UN Habitat, cited)
data_scope.geographic_coverage: Global South focus — illustrative cases from South Asia (India), East Africa, sub-Saharan Africa, South Africa, Uganda
data_scope.urban_mapping_reference: Cattaneo et al. PNAS 2021 — global urban-rural catchment delineation

core_conclusion: Peri-urban zones in the Global South — home to approximately 1 billion people — suffer a predictable service-provision collapse (peri-urban turbulence, PUT) because they lose rural natural infrastructure before urban engineered infrastructure arrives. The PUT framework, grounded in social-ecological systems theory, frames this dip as a hysteresis loop with detectable early-warning signals (flickering, critical slowing down), and calls for high-resolution longitudinal mapping of urbanization frontiers akin to annual deforestation monitoring.
why_it_matters: Reframes 1 billion peri-urban residents as a distinct and analytically invisible population; mechanistically explains service gaps via infrastructure transition dynamics; links urbanization to global infectious-disease risk; proposes systems-science early-warning tools; reveals how wealth stratification converts a universal service dip into structural inequality.
why_nature: Cross-disciplinary synthesis (ecology + urban studies) at global scale; addresses SDG 11 with mechanistic depth; sustainability treated as a dynamic transition process rather than a static state; strong policy-actionability for international development agencies and urban planners.
added_value_to_my_research: PUT turbulence index from satellite + mobile data -> spatiotemporal exposure surface for health geography and peri-urban 2SFCA catchment delineation
Governance network community detection along rural-peri-urban gradient -> institutional driver of service gaps complementing physical accessibility metrics
Flood exposure overlay on PUT zones -> compound-vulnerability demand weight for R2SFCA in climate-exposed cities
PUT hysteresis curve as physics-informed prior in GNN -> prevents spurious monotone density-access predictions for peri-urban health facility modelling
Informal ruralization (urban agriculture geotagged data) as proxy for green-loop re-emergence -> multi-source data fusion stratification for health and accessibility studies

related.title: Mapping urban slums and their inequality in sub-Saharan Africa
related.relation: geospatial delineation of vulnerable peri-urban/slum communities — empirical operationalization of PUT frontier zones

related.title: Urban sprawl is associated with reduced access and increased costs of water and sanitation
related.relation: quantifies how urban form and expansion affect water/sanitation access across 100+ Global South cities — direct empirical test of PUT predictions

related.title: Disproportionate flood exposure for slum populations of the Global South
related.relation: flood risk as an amplifier of PUT-type service collapse — empirical test of the flickering hypothesis for sanitation under rainfall stress

related.title: Natural infrastructure in sustaining global urban freshwater ecosystem services
related.relation: green-loop natural infrastructure for freshwater provision globally — quantifies the ecosystem-service layer that PUT argues collapses in peri-urban transition

related.title: The role of informal ruralization within China's rapid urbanization
related.relation: empirical evidence of the ruralization arm of PUT's hysteresis loop — informal urban agriculture as a green-loop re-emergence signal

related.title: The urban south and the predicament of global sustainability
related.relation: companion framing of Global South urban challenges as statistically and structurally distinct from high-income-country urbanization — contextualizes PUT scope

figures.caption: Infrastructure levels vary across rural, peri-urban, and urban areas. Access to services varies within each zone (arrows); nature acts as a safety net in rural areas of the Global South (green line). The peri-urban trough represents peri-urban turbulence.

Conceptual hysteresis model of urbanization and ruralization: vertical axis = transition from natural/social to engineered infrastructure; horizontal axis = population density. The loop illustrates the service gap in the peri-urban space between rural and urban dynamic equilibria.
